unit.name: Regularization: intuition, penalties, and weight preferences
unit.description: Introduces regularization as the second loss term and explains why L1 and L2 encode different solution preferences.
unit.summary: Regularization is introduced as a term that prevents the model from doing too well on the training data, intentionally trading worse training fit for better test or unseen-data performance [ts=714s]. A toy example contrasts f1, which fits every training point, with f2, which does not fit perfectly but is expected to generalize better [ts=787s]. This is connected to Occam's Razor: among competing hypotheses, prefer the simpler one first [ts=854s]. The lambda parameter is the regularization-strength hyperparameter; lambda = 0 gives no regularization and larger values strengthen the penalty [ts=882s]. L2 squares and sums weights, L1 sums absolute values, and elastic net combines L1 and L2 [ts=923s]. L2 tends to prefer many small nonzero weights, while L1 tends to push dispensable weights to zero [ts=976s]. In the W1/W2 class example, W2=[0.25,0.25,0.25,0.25] and W1=[1,0,0,0] give the same score, but L2 prefers W2 because its squared penalty is lower [ts=1185s]. For the same example, L1 gives both weights equal penalty even though L1 often encourages sparsity in practice [ts=1241s].

key_points.text: Regularization is added to data loss to reduce overfitting: worse training performance can improve test performance.
A simpler function that does not fit every training point can generalize better than a function that fits all training data perfectly.
Lambda controls regularization strength and is tuned as a hyperparameter.
L2 regularization squares and sums weights; L1 regularization sums absolute weight values.
L2 prefers spread-out small weights; L1 tends to encourage sparsity.
For W1=[1,0,0,0] and W2=[0.25,0.25,0.25,0.25], L2 prefers W2, while L1 gives equal penalty in this exact setup.

kp_catalog.name: Linear classifier scores and loss functions
kp_catalog.description: Linear score functions, Softmax/cross-entropy, and dataset loss as an average of per-example losses.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because linear classifier scores and loss functions formalizes how linear score functions, Softmax/cross-entropy, and dataset loss as an average of per-example losses, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Regularization and generalization
kp_catalog.description: The idea that more neurons increase expressive power and overfitting risk, so regularization—not shrinking the architecture by default—should control generalization. The view that very large networks can fit training data closely yet still generalize when regularization is chosen appropriately.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because regularization and generalization clarifies the idea that more neurons increase expressive power and overfitting risk, so regularization—not shrinking the architecture by default—should control generalization, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical, high, and medium.

kp_catalog.name: L1, L2, elastic net, and lambda
kp_catalog.description: Common regularization penalties and the lambda hyperparameter controlling penalty strength.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because l1, L2, elastic net, and lambda clarifies common regularization penalties and the lambda hyperparameter controlling penalty strength, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Weight-preference effects of regularization
kp_catalog.description: Regularizers encode preferences such as L2 spread-out weights and L1 sparsity.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because weight-preference effects of regularization clarifies regularizers encode preferences such as L2 spread-out weights and L1 sparsity, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
